worth_it_explanation: Blocking controls decide which advertisers, product categories, and domains are allowed to compete for a publisher's inventory, so they sit directly on the line between protecting the audience experience and protecting revenue. A block list that drifts out of date can let unwanted demand through, and a block list that grew too wide can suppress fill on inventory that would have cleared cleanly. This check is a review of the block lists and brand safety rules a publisher already holds in Magnite, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete list entry the publisher can keep, narrow, or remove.
intro: Creative blocking is how a publisher tells Magnite which advertisers, product categories, and domains may not run against their inventory. The controls live in two places that work together, namely the block list of advertiser domains and the brand safety rules that decide how those domains are treated per inventory object. This subcategory is about whether those controls are deliberate and current, so the block lists reflect the publisher's actual standards rather than entries that were added once and never revisited. The checks confirm that the blocking is doing real work, both that nothing unwanted is slipping through and that the blocks are not so broad they quietly cut fill on inventory that was never a concern.

audit_checks.title: Advertiser, category, and domain block lists are not maintained to current standards
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Block lists that are not reviewed drift away from the publisher's standards in both directions. Entries the publisher no longer cares about stay in place and suppress demand, while new advertisers or categories that should be blocked never get added and run unchecked. A list set once and left alone stops reflecting the standards it was meant to enforce.

educate.base:
Magnite gives a publisher more than one layer of blocking control. A block list of advertiser domains names the individual advertisers a publisher does not want running against their inventory, and a reusable Block Advertiser Domain List lets the publisher append a maintained set of those domains to an inventory object in bulk rather than entering them one at a time. On top of the domain lists, Advanced Brand Safety Rules decide how a domain is treated, blocked or allowed, against a particular inventory object. Category-level controls draw on a shared classification of ad products and content so a publisher can keep out a whole class of advertiser, for example gambling or a competing service, rather than chasing individual domains.

The list itself is only as good as the last time someone checked it against the publisher's standards. Advertiser domains change, new advertisers enter the auction, product categories the publisher wants to keep out shift over time, and a sponsorship or a direct deal may create a fresh competitive-separation request that the block list has to honor. A list that was built once and left in place stops matching the standards it was meant to enforce, so the audit question is whether the blocking controls reflect a current, documented set of standards or a snapshot from an earlier point.

The classification that category blocking relies on is itself versioned. The IAB Tech Lab Content Taxonomy and Ad Product Taxonomy give the shared language for describing content and the product being advertised, and both have moved through new releases that expand and remap their categories. A category block written against an older version can map imperfectly once the underlying taxonomy advances, so part of keeping the lists current is confirming the category controls still line up with the taxonomy in force.

A few signs point to block lists that are not maintained:
  • No record exists of when the advertiser, category, and domain block lists were last reviewed against the publisher's standards
  • The lists name advertisers or categories that no longer match current concerns, or omit ones the publisher now wants blocked
  • Category controls were written against an earlier taxonomy version and have not been checked against the current one
  • Competitive-separation requests from recent direct deals are not reflected in the block lists
You may want to confirm that the block lists were reviewed against a current, written standard rather than carried forward unchanged. One approach is to set a review cadence and reconcile each list against the publisher's standards and the taxonomy version in force.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, editorial standards often rule out whole categories of advertiser, for example a news outlet keeping out political or gambling demand, and those standards can change with editorial leadership or a single high-profile incident. You may want to reconcile the category and domain block lists against the current editorial standard rather than the one that was in place when the lists were first built.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, the block list usually has to honor competitive separation, keeping out advertisers that compete with the site's own products or with a sponsor's. You may want to confirm the block list reflects the current set of competing advertisers and sponsor agreements, given that the merchandising mix and the deals behind it shift over time.

learn_more.source_title: Glossary (Block Advertiser Domains, Block Advertiser Domain List, Advanced Brand Safety Rule)
Ad Product Taxonomy enabling stronger publisher controls over programmatic ad delivery

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's detailed step-by-step documentation for configuring block lists and Advanced Brand Safety Rules is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the advertiser domain, category, and reusable block lists in Magnite alongside any written standard they are meant to enforce, and look for a record of when each was last reviewed.

magnite_console.label: Magnite console
steps: Open the publisher controls and list the active Block Advertiser Domain Lists and the inventory objects each is appended to.
Open the Advanced Brand Safety Rules and record which domains and categories each rule blocks or allows, and against which inventory.
For each list and rule, record the date it was last edited or reviewed, if that information is available, and which taxonomy version any category control was written against.

Ask the publisher for the written standard the lists are meant to enforce, then compare the entries against it and note any advertiser or category that is present but no longer a concern, or missing but now in scope.

magnite_reporting.label: Magnite reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by advertiser domain (aDomain) showing which advertisers won and rendered against the inventory over a recent period.
Cross-reference the winning advertisers against the block lists and the publisher's standards to see whether any advertiser that should be blocked is still running.
Note any advertiser or category that the standard rules out but the reporting shows still served.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each block list and brand safety rule, the date it was last reviewed, the standard it is meant to enforce, and the taxonomy version behind any category control. Lists that reconcile to a current written standard, with a review on record and category controls aligned to the taxonomy in force, pass. Lists with no review record, entries that no longer match the standard, omissions of advertisers or categories now in scope, or category controls written against a superseded taxonomy are a fail recorded with the specific list and entries affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each block list, its last review date, and how it reconciles to the publisher's standard above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the advertiser, category, and domain block lists for {{site_name}} are not maintained to a current standard, with no review on record and entries that no longer match the publisher's standards. Block lists drift in both directions when they are left unchecked, letting unwanted demand through where new advertisers were never added and suppressing demand where stale entries remain. {{voice_recommend}} reconciling each Block Advertiser Domain List and Advanced Brand Safety Rule in Magnite against a current written standard, confirming category controls align with the IAB Tech Lab taxonomy version in force, and setting a review cadence so the lists stay matched to the standards they enforce. After the review, recheck the winning advertisers in reporting to confirm nothing the standard rules out is still serving.

audit_checks.title: Block lists are over-broad and suppress revenue without cause
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A block list that grew wider than the publisher's standards require removes demand that would have cleared cleanly, which lowers competition in the auction and the price each impression reaches. Broad category blocks, duplicated entries, and blocks applied account-wide when only one placement needed them all cut fill without protecting the audience, so the list trades revenue for a safety margin that was not asked for.

educate.base:
Blocking protects the audience experience, and it also has a cost. Every advertiser, category, or domain a publisher blocks is demand removed from the auction, which lowers the number of bidders competing for the impression and the price the impression can reach. A block list scoped to the publisher's real standards pays for itself by keeping out demand that would harm the audience or breach a deal. A block list that grew wider than those standards require pays a different bill, namely fill and revenue given up on demand that was never a problem.

Over-broad blocking takes a few shapes. A category block can be drawn so widely that it sweeps in advertisers the publisher would have accepted, for example blocking an entire industry to keep out one bad actor. A reusable domain list can carry duplicates or entries copied from another publisher's standards that do not fit this inventory. A block meant for one sensitive placement can be applied account-wide through an Advanced Brand Safety Rule that targets more inventory than it needed to. In each case the block keeps working as written, so nothing looks broken, and the lost demand is invisible unless someone measures it.

Magnite reporting can surface the cost. By reading fill rate and average price on inventory that carries heavy blocking against comparable inventory that carries less, a publisher can see whether the blocking is depressing yield beyond what the standard justifies. The audit question is not whether the publisher should block. Protecting the audience is the point. The question is whether each block earns its place or whether the list has accumulated breadth that costs revenue without a standard behind it.

A few signs point to over-broad blocking:
  • Category blocks cover entire industries where the publisher's concern was a small subset of advertisers
  • Reusable domain lists contain duplicates, or entries inherited from another context that do not map to this publisher's standards
  • A block that one placement required is applied account-wide rather than scoped to the placement that needed it
  • Inventory under heavy blocking shows lower fill and price than comparable inventory, with no standard explaining the gap
You may want to confirm that each block traces back to a documented standard and is scoped no wider than that standard requires. One approach is to compare yield on heavily blocked inventory against comparable inventory, then narrow or remove blocks that cost revenue without a standard behind them.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a single sensitive section, for example a story on a tragedy, may justify tight blocking, but applying that same blocking across the whole site cuts demand on sections that never needed it. You may want to scope the block to the section that required it rather than account-wide, so the rest of the inventory keeps its full pool of demand.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a competitive-separation block belongs on the pages where the conflict exists, for example a product category page, not across editorial inventory that no sponsor cares about. You may want to confirm each separation block is scoped to the inventory the deal actually covers rather than blanketed across the site.

learn_more.source_title: Glossary (Block Advertiser Domains, Block Advertiser Domain List, Advanced Brand Safety Rule, aDomain)
Ad Product Taxonomy enabling stronger publisher controls over programmatic ad delivery

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's detailed step-by-step documentation for scoping Advanced Brand Safety Rules to specific inventory objects is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the scope of each block list and brand safety rule against the standard behind it, and read the yield on heavily blocked inventory against comparable inventory to see whether the blocking is costing more than the standard requires.

magnite_console.label: Magnite console
steps: Open the publisher controls and record the scope of each Block Advertiser Domain List and Advanced Brand Safety Rule, noting which inventory objects each one covers.
For each block, identify the standard or request behind it and whether the scope matches that standard or reaches wider inventory than it needed to.
Check the reusable domain lists for duplicate entries and for entries that do not trace to this publisher's standards.
Flag any category block that covers a whole industry where the publisher's concern was a smaller subset, and any placement-level concern applied account-wide.

magnite_reporting.label: Magnite reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by inventory object with fill rate and average eCPM, separating inventory under heavy blocking from comparable inventory under lighter blocking.
Compare fill and price across the two to see whether the blocked inventory yields less, and by how much, than comparable inventory would predict.
Note any case where heavy blocking lines up with depressed yield and no standard explains the gap, which points to a block worth narrowing.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each block, the standard behind it, the inventory it is scoped to, and the fill and price on that inventory against comparable inventory. Blocks that trace to a documented standard and are scoped no wider than the standard requires pass, even where they cost some demand, on the grounds that the cost is justified. Blocks with no standard behind them, scope wider than the standard needs, duplicate or inherited entries, or a clear yield gap on heavily blocked inventory with no explanation are a fail recorded with the specific block and the revenue it appears to cost.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each block, the standard behind it, its scope, and the fill and price on the inventory it covers above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the block lists for {{site_name}} are scoped wider than the publisher's standards require, so demand that would have cleared cleanly is being removed from the auction. Over-broad blocking lowers the number of bidders competing for each impression and the price it reaches, and the lost fill stays invisible unless it is measured against comparable inventory. {{voice_recommend}} tracing each block back to a documented standard, narrowing category blocks that sweep in advertisers the publisher would accept, removing duplicate or inherited domain entries, and scoping placement-level blocks to the inventory the standard actually covers rather than account-wide. After the change, recheck fill rate and average price on the affected inventory to confirm the blocking now matches the standard without costing revenue it does not need to.
